# face2face — Face Reconstruction (3DSMC)

Analysis-by-synthesis 3D face reconstruction: fit the **Basel Face Model 2017**
to **Pandora** RGB-D frames by energy minimisation, then do expression transfer.
See `Q&A session Ex3, Face Reconstruction.pptx.pdf` for the full project description.






## Dev container (build once, connect with VS Code)

The C++ libraries (Eigen, OpenCV, HDF5) live in the image, so every teammate gets
identical versions. HighFive (header-only) is cloned once by the Makefile.

1. Install Docker + the VS Code **Dev Containers** extension.
2. Open this folder in VS Code → command palette → **"Dev Containers: Reopen in Container"**.
   The first build takes a few minutes; afterwards it is instant.
3. Build + run the C++ target inside the container:
   ```bash
   make run                            # build -> build/face_recon, then run
   make run NUM_IMAGES=20 RGBD=false   # choose how many frames + RGB-only
   ```

`data/` is bind-mounted (not copied into the image), so the licensed datasets
stay out of the image and out of git.

## Build & run the C++ pipeline

All commands run from the **project root** (inside the dev container).

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `make` | Compile only → `build/face_recon` (clones HighFive into `third_party/` on first run) |
| `make run` | Compile, then run with the defaults (`NUM_IMAGES=1`, `RGBD=true`) |
| `make run NUM_IMAGES=20 RGBD=false` | Same, but load 20 frames, RGB only (skip depth) |
| `make clean` | Delete `build/` |
| `./build/face_recon --images 5 --rgbd true` | Run the built binary directly (same flags) |

**The two knobs**
- `NUM_IMAGES` — how many Pandora frames to load (default `1`).
- `RGBD` — `true` = RGB + depth, `false` = RGB only.

**Output** — writes the two BFM faces (now with per-vertex albedo colour) to
`data/out/mean_face.obj` and `data/out/tweaked_face.obj`, and prints the loaded
frame sizes. Open the `.obj` files in any mesh viewer (e.g. MeshLab).

> Needs the datasets in `data/` first — see [SETUP.md](SETUP.md).



## Python proof-of-concept

Validates the pipeline end-to-end *before* the native C++ implementation. Uses
the depth (RGB-D) modality: it fits the BFM to the depth point cloud with
ICP + Levenberg-Marquardt, predicts the face parameters over N iterations, and
renders input-vs-reconstruction.

```bash
# from the project root (works on the host with anaconda, or in the container):
python3 python/check_bfm.py     # sanity-check the BFM .h5 (run this first)
python3 python/main.py          # full PoC -> data/out/fit_result.png
```

Parameters live in `python/config.yaml` (`fit.n_iters`, `fit.n_shape_coeffs`,
`fit.regularization`, `dataset.frame_index`).



### Basel Face Model 2017 — `data/bfm/model2017-1_bfm_nomouth.h5`



| HDF5 path                  | shape          | meaning                                   |
|----------------------------|----------------|-------------------------------------------|
| `shape/model/mean`         | `(159447,)`    | mean geometry, `3·N`, order `[x,y,z,...]` (mm) |
| `shape/model/pcaBasis`     | `(159447,199)` | identity PCA basis (orthonormal)          |
| `shape/model/pcaVariance`  | `(199,)`       | variances; **σ = √variance**              |
| `expression/model/*`       | `(...,100)`    | expression model (δ)                      |
| `color/model/*`            | `(...,199)`    | per-vertex albedo model (β)               |
| `shape/representer/cells`  | `(3,105694)`   | triangle vertex indices (topology)        |

- **N = 53149 vertices, 105694 triangles.** Geometry is in **millimetres**.
- A face is `mean + pcaBasis · (α ⊙ σ)`, with α standard-normal.
- σ is the std-dev you divide by in the regulariser `E_reg` (paper Eq. 5).
- There is also a `metadata/landmarks/text` string with named landmark points —
  useful later for the sparse landmark term.

### Pandora — `data/pandora/base_1_ID01/` (one sequence, a subset)

**Microsoft Kinect One (Time-of-Flight)**. Layout of one sequence:

```
base_1_ID01/
├── RGB/     000000_RGB.png   ... 000804_RGB.png   (805 frames)
├── DEPTH/   000000_DEPTH.png ... 000804_DEPTH.png (805 frames)
├── data.json
└── data.txt
```

| Item    | Format                                                                    |
|---------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `RGB`   | `1920×1080`, 8-bit **BGRA** (4 channels) — high-res colour camera          |
| `DEPTH` | `512×424`, **16-bit (uint16), millimetres** — ToF depth camera             |
| `data.json` | per frame: `frame_num`, `joints` (2D, depth pixels), `joints3D` (3D, metres, camera frame) |
| `data.txt`  | tab-separated per frame: `timestamp`, `frame`, head pose (position + orientation quaternion + Euler°), shoulder pose, then the 2D joints. Use `data.json` for clean access; see the official Pandora readme for exact column semantics. |



#### Camera geometry (depth) This part is fully AI not sure what is meant here!

RGB and DEPTH come from **two different cameras** and are **not registered** to
each other. The depth-camera intrinsics were derived from this dataset's own
2D↔3D joint correspondences (reprojection error < 0.1 px):

```
fx = 367.28   fy = 368.35   cx = 256.66   cy = 210.05
```

Projection / back-projection (image y points down, world y up):

```
u = fx·X/Z + cx          X = (u−cx)/fx · Z
v = fy·(−Y/Z) + cy       Y = −(v−cy)/fy · Z      Z = depth_mm / 1000
```

### Preprocessing summary

- **BFM:** parse the `.h5` once into matrices and cache them (parsing 325 MB of
  HDF5 every run is slow). Generate geometry via `mean + basis·(α⊙σ)`.
- **Pandora depth:** read 16-bit mm, drop `0` pixels, back-project to a metric
  point cloud, crop the head, keep the front-facing surface (the face).
- **RGB↔depth:** if you later use the photometric term you must register the two
  cameras (we only have depth intrinsics here) — that is a known open task.
